story:
The sun rises over a lush forest where birds hop and sing, light splinters across green leaves, and animals drink from a clear, zigzagging river. High above, Lily—a slender willow switch crowned with the brightest leaf—glistens with dew and waves like a queen. Down by the bank sits Silly—a lump of clay with a small head, stubby neck, and no limbs. Ugly as a misfit potato, he doesn’t mind. With hopelessly hopeful eyes, he gazes upward, utterly in love with Lily. Seasons circle: summer blazes; autumn strips her leaves; winter seals the world in snow. When the rain returns, Silly smiles. He dreams once: arms, legs, a voice. He climbs, kisses Lily, rides a bird, glides on a cloud, slides a rainbow, sails a swan. Just as their noses touch, an earthquake splits the dream; he wakes—still clay, still silent—but Lily is back, shining.

Then comes the roar: brum-brum-brum-brrrrrr. Chainsaws gnash, trees crash, birds scream, beasts stampede. Silly wishes for limbs that won’t come. A great tree falls and, in the hush after the bang, Lily herself—torn free—lands an inch away, tears jeweled on her face. She hears him—mind to mind. They banter—names, blushes, a flustered “blah, blah, blah” to hide love he can’t say out loud. Machines strip branches and torch piles. Smoke smothers. Lily coughs for help. Silly prays. A deep voice answers: You just need to move your butt.

He tries. Nothing. He remembers hatchling turtles clawing to light, a fawn rising while its mother holds back wolves. The universe won’t move him; he must. He says, I am ready, and wind rises, ash shifts, and he rolls. Lily is blown downhill, scraping for grip, finally hooking her base into a trench by the river. Silly bumps into her. “You stepped on my root!” she yelps. “Sorry,” he says into her mind, flustered. Eyes locked, they confess in code: “I blah, blah, blah you.” The storm howls. Thunder cracks. Rain lashes. Lily bends over him; Silly softens and settles into her trench, holding fast. The river swells, cold and strong. “Do you know why I called myself Silly?” he asks silently, smiling inside. Because I believed we would end up together. Lily leans forward; noses meet; her rose-like scent fills him. He molds tight around her base. A wave blankets them; still they cling. Sun returns. A rainbow hangs like a shield. Lily shakes out her shoulders; her few leaves glitter. She leans over the water, braced at last.

Night brings grief: a home cut down, family gone. Yet today she met her savior, her love, her companion. At dawn the deep voice returns: Your old home is gone. Build your own—right here, right now. I gave you Silly. Whenever you’re ready, rise and shine. Lily breathes, I am ready, and begins to root into Silly’s clay. Days unfurl; by next year she is a foot-tall sapling. Across the scarred land, millions more rise, returning life to the forest.

Years pass. Trees stand tall and wide; birds trill between crowns; animals graze; the river runs clear. At the bank, a sun-baked nub of Silly still gazes up at Lily’s diamond-bright sway atop the tallest tree.

From afar, the old buzz returns: brum-brum-brum-brrrrrr. A warning, a plea: God made trees friends to human beings. Be kind to your friends.
